videos.title: Intro to Provisions for the Hereafter: What Will You Take With You When You Die?
videos.playlist_title: Provisions for the Hereafter with Sh. Ali
videos.suggested_title: What You Take With You When You Die: Introduction to Zad al-Ma'ad
videos.catchy_title: What survives when your body decays?
videos.summary: The speaker shares a touching story about Jabir ibn Abdullah, a companion of the Prophet who traveled a month's journey to hear a hadith directly from another companion, illustrating the companions' prioritization of knowledge. He emphasizes that when a person dies, the only thing they take with them is their knowledge, understanding of Allah, beliefs, and deeds—not material possessions. The speaker introduces a weekly Tuesday evening class (6-7 PM) that studies Zad al-Ma'ad (Provisions for the Day of the Great Appointment) by Ibn al-Qayyim, a comprehensive work on the Prophet Muhammad's life, teachings, character, and practices, available for free via live streaming on Facebook.

videos.title: Zadul-Ma’ad (Provisions for the Hereafter) with Sh. Ali Mashhour  - Part 1
videos.playlist_title: Provisions for the Hereafter with Sh. Ali
videos.suggested_title: Introduction to Zadul-Ma'ad: The Life and Teachers of Ibn al-Qayyim al-Jawziyyah - Part 1
videos.catchy_title: Who Was Ibn al-Qayyim? Understanding His Teachers and Legacy
videos.summary: This is the first part of a comprehensive course on Zadul-Ma'ad (Provisions for the Hereafter), a classical Islamic text by Ibn al-Qayyim al-Jawziyyah. Sheikh Ali Mashhour begins by establishing the importance of understanding an author's background before studying their work. He provides an extensive biographical overview of Ibn al-Qayyim, detailing his full name (Muhammad ibn Abu Bakr), his lineage, birthdate in 691 AH, and his formative education in Damascus. The lecture emphasizes Ibn al-Qayyim's most influential teachers: his father Abu Bakr, Sheikh al-Islam Ibn Taymiyyah (with whom he spent 16 years), and Imam al-Mizzi, the foremost hadith scholar of his era. The speaker also mentions some of Ibn al-Qayyim's notable students, including Ibn Kathir and Ibn Rajab al-Hanbali, illustrating the interconnected chain of Islamic scholarship.

videos.title: Zadul-Ma’ad (Provisions for the Hereafter) with Sh. Ali Mashhour  -  Part 2
videos.playlist_title: Provisions for the Hereafter with Sh. Ali
videos.suggested_title: Allah's Wisdom in Choosing the Prophets: Understanding Divine Selection and Discretion
videos.summary: This is the second part of a series on Zadul-Ma'ad (Provisions for the Hereafter) by Ibn Qayyim al-Jawziyyah, presented by Sheikh Ali Mashhour. The lecture focuses on understanding the significance of the Prophet Muhammad's era and the foundational concepts of the book. The speaker emphasizes three main topics: Allah's sole right to create and choose from His creation, the criteria for Allah's choice being His infinite wisdom and knowledge, and the importance of understanding and following the Prophet's Sunnah. Through Quranic verses from Surat al-Qasas, Surat al-An'am, and Surat az-Zukhruf, the speaker addresses how Allah responds to the antagonists who questioned why Muhammad was chosen as the messenger. The lecture establishes that Allah's discretion in choosing His messengers and distributing His mercy is based on perfect knowledge and wisdom, not on human preferences or social status, and that attempting to interfere with this divine choice constitutes a form of shirk.

videos.title: Zadul-Ma’ad (Provisions for the Hereafter) with Sh. Ali Mashhour  -  Part 3
videos.playlist_title: Provisions for the Hereafter with Sh. Ali
videos.suggested_title: The Prophet's Method of Ablution (Wudu): Detailed Practice and Sunnah
videos.catchy_title: How the Prophet Made Ablution: The Complete Sunnah
videos.summary: This lecture is Part 3 of Shaykh Ali Mashhour's series on Zadul-Ma'ad (Provisions for the Hereafter) by Ibn Qayyim al-Jawziyyah. The session begins by reviewing previous discussions on Allah's choice in creating and elevating whom He wills, and how understanding this eliminates jealousy and envy from the heart. The bulk of the lecture focuses on the methodology and practice of wudu (ablution) as performed by the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him), drawing from the Quranic instruction in Surah Al-Ma'idah. The shaykh details the Prophet's various practices regarding wudu, including how frequently he performed it, the amount of water used (approximately one mud, about 650ml), the number of times he washed each limb (once, twice, or three times), and the specific manner of rinsing the mouth and nose together.

videos.title: Zadul-Ma’ad (Provisions for the Hereafter) with Sh. Ali Mashhour  -  Part 4
videos.playlist_title: Provisions for the Hereafter with Sh. Ali
videos.suggested_title: The Six Pillars of Ablution (Wudu) According to Zadul-Ma'ad - Islamic Jurisprudence and Prophetic Practice
videos.catchy_title: What Makes Ablution Valid and Complete?
videos.summary: This lecture continues the study of Zadul-Ma'ad, Ibn Al-Qayyim's comprehensive work on the Prophet Muhammad's traditions and practices. The session focuses on the pillars and conditions of ablution (wudu), establishing that there are six essential pillars: washing the face, washing the hands and arms to the elbows, wiping the head, washing the feet to the ankles, maintaining the proper order of these actions, and performing them in quick succession as a single unit. The speaker emphasizes that this order is derived from Quranic instruction in Surah Al-Ma'idah and is consistently demonstrated throughout the Prophet's sunnah, establishing these elements as obligatory components of valid ablution.

videos.title: Zadul-Ma’ad (Provisions for the Hereafter) with Sh. Ali Mashhour  -  Part 5
videos.playlist_title: Provisions for the Hereafter with Sh. Ali
videos.suggested_title: The Prophet's Wudu: Beard Care, Footwear Wiping, and Practical Details
videos.catchy_title: Why the Prophet's beard matters for your wudu
videos.summary: This session from Zadul-Ma'ad explores the practical details of the Prophet Muhammad's ablution (wudu) practices, focusing on specific body parts and circumstances. The lecture covers the Prophet's thick beard and how he would occasionally run wet fingers through it, establishing scholarly principles for beard washing based on skin visibility. It discusses hand and toe washing, the importance of water reaching all areas, and the use of the Prophet's seal ring during ablution. A substantial portion addresses wiping over footwear (khuff and socks), including the time limits for residents (24 hours) and travelers (72 hours), with detailed examples clarifying when the wiping period begins and how changing footwear affects the ruling.

videos.title: Zadul-Ma’ad (Provisions for the Hereafter) with Sh. Ali Mashhour  -  Part 6
videos.playlist_title: Provisions for the Hereafter with Sh. Ali
videos.suggested_title: Tayammum: The Alternative to Wudu When Water Is Unavailable
videos.catchy_title: When Water Isnt Available, What Do You Do?
videos.summary:
This lecture is Part 6 of a series on Zadul-Ma'ad (Provisions for the Hereafter) by Ibn Qayyim al-Jawziyyah, focusing on tayammum as an alternative to wudu. The speaker explains that tayammum is performed when water is unavailable or inaccessible, or when a person is unable to use available water due to illness, extreme cold, or other valid reasons. Tayammum involves patting the ground once with the intention, then wiping the face and hands up to the wrist.

The lecture details the specific conditions and methodology of tayammum based on Quranic verses and the Prophet's Sunnah. The speaker emphasizes that tayammum can be performed using any pure substance that rests on the ground (dust, dirt, sand, or even dust on surfaces), and discusses Ibn Qayyim's reasoning that the Prophet must have used sand during desert travels when water was scarce. The lecture also addresses common misconceptions about tayammum, clarifying that it is performed only once (not three times like wudu), and that it is valid whenever prayer time arrives, regardless of location.

videos.title: Zadul-Ma’ad (Provisions for the Hereafter) with Sh. Ali Mashhour  -  Part 7
videos.playlist_title: Provisions for the Hereafter with Sh. Ali
videos.suggested_title: Purification in Islam: Wudu, Tayammum, and Ghusl - Invalidators and Proper Methods
videos.summary: This lecture covers detailed Islamic jurisprudence on purification (taharah), specifically focusing on the invalidators of wudu (ablution), the rulings surrounding tayammum (dry ablation), and the proper method of ghusl (full body ritual washing). The speaker discusses common misconceptions about these practices, including what breaks wudu (such as touching private parts, eating camel meat, and deep sleep), the conditions under which ghusl is obligatory (sexual impurity, menstruation, and postpartum bleeding), and clarifies that a regular shower only counts as ghusl if done with the explicit intention of worship. The lecture emphasizes that ghusl performed for ritual purification replaces the need for wudu, and provides the sunnah method of ghusl as practiced by the Prophet Muhammad, while also explaining the minimum requirements for validity.

videos.title: Zadul-Ma’ad (Provisions for the Hereafter) with Sh. Ali Mashhour  -  Part 8
videos.playlist_title: Provisions for the Hereafter with Sh. Ali
videos.suggested_title: The Prophet's Method of Prayer: Hand Positions, Takbir, and Correcting Common Misconceptions
videos.catchy_title: How Did the Prophet Actually Pray?
videos.summary: This lecture is Part 8 of Zadul-Ma'ad (Provisions for the Hereafter), focusing on the physical mechanics and methodology of prayer (Salah) according to the Sunnah of Prophet Muhammad. Sheikh Ali Mashhour begins by emphasizing the importance of patience in seeking knowledge and explains that while there are differences of opinion on prayer details, the core principles are agreed upon. The main content covers how the Prophet initiated prayer with Takbir (Allahu Akbar), the proper raising of hands to ear or shoulder level, the placement of the right hand over the left, and addresses misconceptions such as verbalizing intention before prayer, which has no authentic basis. The lecturer clarifies anatomical positions for hand placement and explains what is and is not permissible in prayer.

videos.title: Zadul-Ma’ad (Provisions for the Hereafter) with Sh. Ali Mashhour  -  Part 9
videos.playlist_title: Provisions for the Hereafter with Sh. Ali
videos.suggested_title: Understanding Worship vs. Customs and the Proper Methodology for Seeking Islamic Knowledge
videos.catchy_title: Building Your Knowledge Like a Muscle
videos.summary: This is part 9 of a series on Zadul-Ma'ad (Provisions for the Hereafter) by the late Islamic scholar, led by Sheikh Ali Mashhour. The lecture begins with a foundational hadith from both Sahih Bukhari and Sahih Muslim about Allah granting understanding of the religion to those destined for good, explaining that this applies to all believers acquiring knowledge relevant to their circumstances. Sheikh Mashhour then discusses the crucial distinction between worship ('ibadah) and customs ('adat): worship requires explicit Islamic evidence and permission, while customs are permissible unless prohibited. He emphasizes that innovation in worship is strictly forbidden. The second major section addresses the proper methodology for seeking Islamic knowledge, stressing consistency over intensity, depth over breadth, and the importance of developing one's learning capacity gradually like building a muscle. He warns against the common pattern of intense study bursts followed by complete cessation, and advocates for daily, focused study of a single subject. Finally, the lecture transitions to discussing the Prophet Muhammad's method of Qur'anic recitation, specifically his elongated and measured recitation style, and the proper way to say 'Amen' during congregational prayer—timing it with the imam's recitation and maintaining unified pronunciation with the congregation.

videos.title: Zadul-Ma’ad (Provisions for the Hereafter) with Sh. Ali Mashhour  -  Part 10
videos.playlist_title: Provisions for the Hereafter with Sh. Ali
videos.suggested_title: Facing the Qibla in Prayer: Historical Context and Practical Application
videos.catchy_title: How Precise Must You Face the Kaaba?
videos.summary: This lecture from the Zadul-Ma'ad series discusses the proper way to face the qibla during prayer. Sheikh Ali Mashhour explains the historical context of why Muslims initially prayed toward Bayt al-Maqdis before the command was changed to face toward the Kaaba in Mecca. He clarifies that scholars differ on the interpretation of facing the qibla—while some require precision toward the Kaaba's exact center, the majority opinion holds that facing the general direction is sufficient, with a 45-degree margin of acceptable deviation. The lecture emphasizes that intricate calculations are unnecessary and that historical mosques built centuries ago, many of which are slightly misaligned, demonstrate the validity of this understanding. The discussion then transitions to the length and content of Quranic recitation in different prayer times, noting that the Prophet would typically recite 60-100 medium-length verses in Fajr prayer, significantly more than contemporary practice.

videos.title: Zadul-Ma’ad (Provisions for the Hereafter) with Sh. Ali Mashhour  -  Part 11
videos.playlist_title: Provisions for the Hereafter with Sh. Ali
videos.hadith_chapter: Chapter on Prayer Lengths and Recitations
videos.suggested_title: The Prophet's Quran Recitation in Daily Prayers: Practices and Wisdom
videos.catchy_title: How long did the Prophet recite in prayer?
videos.summary: This segment from Zadul-Ma'ad (Provisions for the Hereafter) by Ibn al-Qayyim discusses the Prophet Muhammad's recitation practices across the five daily prayers. The speaker explains the typical length and chapters the Prophet would recite in Fajr, Zuhr, Asr, Maghrib, and Isha prayers, using authentic narrations and scholarly analysis. The lecture contextualizes these practices within the daily life and schedules of Medina's inhabitants, explaining why certain prayers featured longer recitations while others were shorter, and how this balanced spiritual devotion with practical community needs.

videos.title: Zadul-Ma’ad (Provisions for the Hereafter) with Sh. Ali Mashhour  -  Part 12
videos.playlist_title: Provisions for the Hereafter with Sh. Ali
videos.suggested_title: Understanding the Prophet's Quranic Recitation in Prayer: Context and Sunnah Practice
videos.catchy_title: Why the Mu'adh hadith is deeply misunderstood
videos.summary: This lecture is Part 12 of a series on Zadul-Ma'ad (Provisions for the Hereafter) by Ibn al-Qayyim, focusing on the Prophet Muhammad's recitation practices during daily prayers, particularly salat al-Isha. The speaker provides crucial context to a commonly misunderstood hadith about Mu'adh ibn Jabal, explaining that the Prophet's rebuke was specific to Mu'adh leading excessively long prayers (2-3 hours) that discouraged his community, not to moderately longer recitations. The lecture clarifies that while the Prophet recited chapters like Surah Al-Baqarah during some prayers, the context involved specific circumstances and audiences. The speaker emphasizes the difference between what is permissible (mubah) and what constitutes the Sunnah (the Prophet's actual practice), explaining that incomplete chapter recitations or merely tail-end portions of chapters were not part of the Prophet's established practice, despite being technically allowed.
